EXT. HARBOR - DAY
The South American harbor is crammed with freight ships. DOCKWORKERS climb the ramps with bags of coffee beans. Cranes unload boxes of Japanese coffeemakers.
EXT. BAR DISTRICT - DAY

The narrow street is lined with garish bars. Half-nude HOOKERS lean over balconies and call down to passing SHIPMATES. More hookers, escaping the tropical heat, sit in doorways and fan themselves to the Salsa MUSIC from all the bars.
INT. RESTAURANT PATIO - DAY

Tapping to the music are a man's feet in sandals and a gold ankle chain. His linen trousers are rolled up in the heat. Dyed in his silk shirt are small faces of Rita Hayworth. He is LEWIS MOLINA, 58, an American who has lived here since jumping ship at 19. His hair is tinted to hide the gray. He has the weathered face of a man who has
As the camera moves from his feet to his face, we hear Molina reading in a rather effeminate voice.
He says he can't come back for Christmas like he promised ... and he's very sorry he hasn't seen his new son yet... and he loves you
with all his heart. Spread across his table are envelopes and stamps. A WOMAN of the district listens to him with quiet dignity. She has a baby on her lap and a four-year-old boy at her side.
So he won't come at all this year. (MORE)
(holds back tears) What about money for the children?
Sorry. He doesn't say. (turns to bar) Gabriel! Did you forget my iced tea? (turns to woman) Do you want to write him back?
The woman is too sad to answer. The boy fiddles with his Star Wars toy. Molina moistens a pen with his tongue and begins to write.
Dear Peter, the boys and I were delighted to get your thoughtful card ...

A ship horn BLARES over the music. As Molina writes, we see the interior of his hang-out and 'office.' The restaurant is divided by a folding partition, painted with jungle leaves and spider webs. One side is a bar with weary dockworkers. The other side is a modest dining room with SHIPS OFFICERS at clean tablecloths. GABRIEL, 35, a handsome waiter in a tunic, moves through the tables with a tray of dirty dishes. His well-dressed WIFE, waiting impatiently at the kitchen window, resumes her harangue.
I told you last week, the deadline is today. If we don't pay by 5:30, the girls will miss a whole semester.
Saying nothing, Gabriel puts an iced tea on his tray.
Are you listening! (MORE)
You know how I struggled to get them accepted by that school!
Gabriel approaches Molina's table on the patio. glances up with a smile.
Your tea, Molina. Sorry it took so long.

That's okay. (returns to letter) Remember, dearest Peter, I am your woman, who misses you more than life itself, who lives only for the blessed moment of your return. I save all my love for you alone. Your beloved -(looks up) What was your name, darling? WOMAN Please, Senhor Molina. sign my own name.
Molina is miffed.
If you think that's better.
The woman pays the fee and leaves. Gabriel, waiting nearby, looks uneasy as he leans forward.

Molina, could I ask you a small favor? It's only a hundred. For my kid's school. (indicates wife) She has the money, but she says a father should provide these things. Molina takes out a hundred, leaving only twenty for
himself. Gabriel accepts it with grateful dignity.
Thank you, Molina. I'll pay you back next month.
Molina watches him approach his angry wife.
Bitch.
EXT. BAR DISTRICT - NIGHT
Although the bars are closed, the narrow street still swelters in the heat. Molina and Gabriel stroll past hookers lingering in the shadows.
I'll never understand. How can a man with your intelligence work in a dump like that?
It's life, Molina.
No, it's a shame. With your looks and charm, you should work in a chic restaurant. In a big international hotel. Making three times what you get in that stinkhole.
You think I haven't tried?
They turn a corner and approach the bus-stop.
Look, when I brought my mother down from Miami, she worked the switchboard at the Copacabana Palace. She still knows the manager. She'll introduce you, and poof. A new life.
Forget it, Lewis. I can't even speak English.
The bus approaches.
(quickly) I'll pay for your lessons. We can practice everyday. It's perfect, you'll learn in no time.
Talk is cheap, eh Molina?
I can afford it. Really.
(boards the bus) See you tomorrow.
Goodnight, Gabriel. Kiss the children for me.
Molina watches the bus pull away. Several TOUGH TEENAGERS stand in front of a run-down movie theater. One BOY, 16, stares at Molina. Their eyes meet.
INT. MOLINA'S APARTMENT - NIGHT
Molina, exhausted, enters his dimly-lit parlor. The late-night TV broadcasts a test pattern. His MOTHER, 81, wakes up on the sofa. They speak in English.
Mama, you should be in bed. Why don't you listen to the doctor? MOTHER What time is it?
Long past your bedtime. I'll help you.
He takes her elbow. a cane. Come, She hobbles toward her bedroom with Converted to PDF by www.screentalk.org
Do you know anyone who teaches English?
Why are you so late, Lewis? something happen?
Of course not, Mama. Just go to sleep.
EXT. BAR/RESTAURANT - DAY
Molina sips coffee at his regular "desk" on the patio. A police car pulls to a stop. A POLICE OFFICER gets out and approaches Molina's table.
Good morning, Molina. How's it going?
Not bad.
The officer sits down and fingers Molina's papers and pens.
How's business?
Slow. I need more coffee.
You want some?
You don't have time. (points) Recognize the kid in the car?
Molina squints at the police car. In the backseat is the 16-year-old boy (from last night) with his FATHER. Converted to PDF by www.screentalk.org
His father's been singing your favorite song. Let's go.
Crestfallen, Molina stands up and gazes back into the restaurant at Gabriel.
EXT. PRISON - DAY The old prison is massive.
by slums. The stone walls are surrounded
INT. PRISON - DAY
The corridors are filthy. paint is peeling. The floors are cracked.
INT. TORTURE ROOM - DAY
Shadows on the wall show a man being tortured. He suddenly falls to the floor. His boots are spattered with blood.
Is he dead? No, I'll wake him up.
Forget it, I'm too tired. Just
haul him back to the cell.
INT. CELL - NIGHT
The stark cell door has a steel door and one small window with bars. Molina sits on a prison bed and speaks directly into the camera. Converted to PDF by www.screentalk.org
When the tycoon discovers where she is working, he buys the hotel
and fires her. Just when she needs to pay for her lover's T.B.
INT. FISHERMAN'S SHACK - NIGHT (MEXICAN MOVIE)
We see the MOVIE that Molina is describing. look like a cheap Mexican melodrama. The images The tubercular YOUNG MAN is asleep on a humble bed in the shanty. VOICE OF MOLINA
him to sleep.
INT. CELL- NIGHT
Now we see that Molina is addressing someone. PANS to the other bed.
The CAMERA
His CELL-MATE wears the same blood-spattered boots seen in the torture room. The CAMERA CONTINUES from boots to head. The cell-mate appears to be sleeping, his face to the wall.
She doesn't dare worry him when he's so sick.

The cell-mate rolls over with his eyes open. He is VALENTIN ARREGUI, 32, thin and unshaven. His arms show the marks of torture. He has the intense look of a man who has been hurt in more ways than one.
Don't bother me. I need to sleep.
INT. FISHERMAN'S SHACK - NIGHT (MEXICAN MOVIE)
Coughing, the tubercular Young Man struggles to his feet and puts on his coat.
One night her lover is feeling a little better and decides to meet her after work at the hotel. Leaving, the young man blows out the candle.
EXT. HOTEL - NIGHT (MEXICAN MOVIE)

The Young Man speaks to the DOORMAN at the large hotel. The Doorman gives him a cigarette and matches. VOICE OF MOLINA The doorman tells him that she hasn't worked there for quite a while. He asks for a cigarette even though he knows smoking is suicide for a man with T.B.
EXT. DARK STREET - NIGHT (MEXICAN MOVIE)

The gaunt Young Man sees several PROSTITUTES under a street lamp, calling out to passing cars. VOICE OF MOLINA In desperation he walks street after street until, finally, he sees her on a corner. She is wearing a gaudy outfit and flirting with an old man in a car. He suddenly realizes what she's been hiding from him: she was selling her body to save his life. The young man, crying, takes a deep drag on his cigarette.
INT. CELL - NIGHT
Molina, playing the role of the young man, pretends to cry with a cigarette. Valentin struggles to his feet and goes to the shit-bucket in the corner.
Do you like it?
Leave me alone, man. I need to build my strength.
Just tell me if you like it.
Valentin, exhausted by torture, leans against the wall as he urinates.
Now that it's over, it seems like the lovers are dead.
It's not over yet, my dear.
Shit.
INT. FISHERMAN'S SHACK - NIGHT (MEXICAN MOVIE)

The WOMAN (who was dressed as a hooker) arrives home in normal clothing. Seeing his empty bed, she rushes into the kitchen. On the table are a plate full of cigarette butts and an empty bottle of rum. Then she sees his note by the window.
Instantly she realizes that he knows her secret. She finds his note by the window.

Moonlight falls on his tearstained words as she reads: "I love you so much that I cannot go on being such a burden to you. Please don't look for me. If God wants us to meet again, we'll find each other without even looking."
The movie music swells: a sad, romantic BOLERO.
INT. CELL - NIGHT
Molina is humming the same Bolero. the ceiling. Valentin stares at
Isn't it wonderful!
It helps pass the time.
Does that mean you like it?
It doesn't help the cause.
Blessed Mary, is that all you can talk about? You must've studied Political Philosophies in school.
Valentin looks at him with disgust.
The Phrase is Political Science, and the answer is -- no, I studied Journalism.
Ah! So you can appreciate a good story.
And easily spot a cheap one.
Don't worry, my dear. Tomorrow morning Mother will show her best. Better than any movie you'll ever see.
No, I can't be listening to this junk during the day. I've got important things to think about.
Tomorrow night, then.
INT. CELL - DAY
Valentin reads a paperback entitled Third World Economics. Molina, like an actor, uses a mirror and lipstick to apply a light rouge on his cheeks.
Tell me about your girlfriend. You do have one, don't you?
Leave me alone.
Of course, you do. What's the big secret? Is she involved in a scandal?
Valentin glares up from his book.
It's none of your business. (turns away) That's my problem.
INT. CELL - NIGHT
Molina is excited to be telling another movie. Converted to PDF by www.screentalk.org
Imagine Paris during the Second World War, such a wonderfully heroic time. Proud soldiers march along
the broad avenues in a magnificent Parade of Liberation.
EXT. PARIS - DAY The Parisian skyline.
marching troops.
SOUNDS of crowds, military drums,
VOICE OF MOLINA The air is charged with excitement as they march under the Arch of Triumph. PAN DOWN the Arch of Triumph to reveal columns of NAZI TROOPS.
Wearing their marvelous uniforms.

Through a series of shots, we move from the parade to an average French working-class neighborhood. The images look like a slapdash German film of the 1940's. More specifically, a Nazi propaganda movie.
Forget what you read in the history books. The truth is, the French welcomed the Germans with open arms.

As two FRENCHMEN unload a truck at a hardware store, a NAZI CONVOY appears. German troops arrest the two men, and find others hiding in the cellar. One is shot trying to escape, the others surrender. They are Jews. Converted to PDF by www.screentalk.org
Oh, there were a few who didn't appreciate them, but for the most part, the French cheered because they were saving France from

disaster and starvation. WERNER, a handsome Gestapo officer, whispers to an AIDE. A neighborhood crowd spits on the smugglers. German troops break open the cargo and distribute the contraband food. A grateful OLD WOMAN kisses Werner's hand. A pickup truck stops in an alley and watches the crowd. VOICE OF MOLINA A small truck watches from nearby. Inside are these two French thugs from the Resistance. The driver is a hulking cross-eyed Flunky. His boss is a grimy little Clubfoot. Their truck is loaded with
INT. CELL - NIGHT
Valentin sits up.
You mean the Nazis are the good guys? Are you crazy?
So what, it's a movie I saw.
Are you saying freedom fighters are bad guys?
Molina, upset, kicks his rather effeminate speech into
Converted to PDF by www.screentalk.org
I don't explain my movies. just ruins the emotion.
(turns away) This movie's already ruined.
Now, the Clubfoot ...
EXT. STREET - DAY (NAZI MOVIE)
The Clubfoot hobbles from his truck to a phone booth and dials.
Let me talk to Michelle.
INT. CABARET - DAY (NAZI MOVIE)
A chorus line rehearses on the stage of a lavish nightclub. MICHELLE, a blonde chorus-girl, runs offstage to answer the phone.
Yes?
(curt) They got Jacob and Bernard. Did you get the map?
No, there was no time to -No time! Or else.
You better come through.
Don't worry, I'll get it. work I'm going to -
Just get it. Nothing else matters. Vive la France.
INT. CABARET NIGHT - NIGHT (NAZI MOVIE) The Blonde dances in the chorus line. Elegant waiters

move along dimly-lit tables of German officers. VOICE OF MOLINA Every night, a ravishing young chanteuse takes the stage and sings to an audience filled with the German High Command. LENI, a stunning brunette dressed in elegant black, appears in the spotlight. VOICE OF MOLINA She is Leni Lamaison. A Grace Kelly with the voice of a Piaf. VOICE OF VALENTIN
Anyway... (sighs) The lyrics portray a sweet innocent nightingale, taken prisoner from
its nest by a fierce brutal eagle. Can you imagine her courage, singing this in public during the German
she descends the stage steps and finishes her song right in front of Werner's table. Werner! A man no woman can resist. Totally

Converted to PDF by www.screentalk.org Leni, singing, moves between tables of Nazi brass who fidget at her bold lyrics. Werner whispers and gives an AIDE his name-card engraved with a swastika. Leni concludes face-to-face with Werner. Their eyes lock. Silent tension fills the cabaret. Werner rises slowly and begins to applaud. The audience bursts into applause.
INT. CELL - NIGHT
Molina, acting out Werner's applause, is totally immersed in his movie. Valentin lies in his bunk.
(sighs) Oh, Valentin, this movie is so wonderful, but I've got to get some sleep. Do you mind?
Molina waits for Valentin's reaction. stares at the door. Valentin just
What's the matter?
Nothing.
Well, something's bothering you.
I can't understand why they stooped my interrogation. It's been three days.
And Mother thought her bedtime story was helping you feel better.
Yeah, a big help.
Converted to PDF by www.screentalk.org
Fine, we'll pick it up tomorrow night. Isn't is romantic?
Valentin gazes at him with tight-lipped anger.
Your Nazis are about as romantic as the fucking Warden. That bastard's been kicking and sticking me more days than I can count, and now I gotta listen to this crap.
I can imagine.
No, you can't. You really like those jackboot blondes.
Of course. I'm in love with Werner. And you know why? (gestures)
He's hung like a horse. Valentin grimaces and rolls over, his face to the wall.
Go to sleep. What's wrong? sex?
Afraid to talk about
You really want to know, Molina? I find you boring. I know you like a book.
Molina, angry, leans forward on his bed.
Darling, you don't know page one. You know I'm a faggot? Well, congratulations. You know I corrupt minors? (MORE)
Well, it's even on TV, film at eleven. You know I go for little
Okay! Okay, Molina. Your movie is terrific.
With naive hope, Molina picks up a candle to see Valentin's face more clearly.
You really mean it?
(looks at him) Sure, it's better than a tampax commercial. Let's mindfuck everybody.
Molina recoils and quickly regroups.
But propaganda is just dandy when it serves your stinking revolution.
Now could you know anything about revolution? You're just a damn queen.
And how could you know anything if you're so damn prejudiced?.
Valentin rolls back toward the wall.

Now what? Our liberated journalist can't even say "no comment"? Has our freedom fighter run into too many fights or too many freedoms? (sighs) Just when my movie made me forget this stinkhole. Why couldn't my cell-mate be Werner? (MORE)
(lays down) Goodnight. Dream about my French Blonde.
(curt) I have my own woman.
Because she fights for your pissass revolution, I'm sure.
No. Because I love her. (pause) I'd hate to imagine who you dream about?
The singer, darling. I'm always the heroine. Sweet dreams.
INT. PRISON CORRIDOR - DAWN
TWO GUARDS drag a limp p body into a cell and prop the NEW PRISONER against a bunk. His head is hooded with a cloth sack. His shirt is blood-stained. SOUND of a muffled groan.
INT. CELL - DAWN
Valentin, kneeling by the door, peers through the foodslot into the cell across the corridor. Molina, drowsy, squints at him.
What's going on?
Converted to PDF by www.screentalk.org
Quiet, they're bringing in someone new.
Valentin SEES: departing. the door being shut and the guards' feet Molina rubs his eyes.
What time is it, anyway?
As Valentin whispers through the food-slot, a voice echoes down the corridor.
Hey... Hey..
Who's looking for trouble? (pause) Then shut up!
(sitting up) Holy Mary, it sounds like a train station. How do they expect me to get any sleep?
Valentin, deeply disturbed, moves toward the wash basin.
He's really bleeding.
A political prisoner?
They don't treat you like that for stealing bananas.
Do you know him?
Valentin begins brushing his teeth.
Converted to PDF by www.screentalk.org INT. CELL - RIGHT
Molina seductively plays the role of Leni.

In her dressing-room, Leni changes into a pink satin gown which makes her look heavenly. Firm breasts. Thin waist. Smooth hips. Her skin smells of fresh lavender -VALENTIN What is this, Nazi propaganda or Nazi porno?
Don't be ridiculous. Sometimes I get a little carried away. (resumes)
All of a sudden, Leni notices a magnificent bouquet of flowers.
Sent by your Gestapo Loverboy, I suppose.
INT. CABARET DRESSING ROOM - NIGHT (NAZI MOVIE)
Leni examines the flowers, finds Werner's name-card and tosses it aside. The Blonde enters the dressing room.
What fabulous flowers!
Who are
Some filthy Kraut.
Leni pats the Blonde's stomach with a sympathetic smile.
Yes , soon I won' t be able to dance. Can you imagine, unemployed and pregnant?
Converted to PDF by www.screentalk.org
And pregnant by a German! BLONDE What can I do, Leni? Love has no country.

The STAGE MANAGER opens the door. STAGE MANAGER Two minutes, Michelle. The Blonde runs. Leni, alone, picks up Werner's name card and stares at the bouquet. SUPER-IMPOSE: The eyes of Werner. VOICE OF MOLINA Werner's eyes, his beautiful blue eyes, began to burn into her soul. Eyes like the claws of an eagle -inescapable.
INT. CELL - NIGHT
Valentin, wrapped in a blanket, shakes his head in disgust.
How can you just sit there and rewrite history like that?
Because this movie is divine, and that's all that counts. If I don't think about nice things, I could go nuts in here -- like Charlotte of Mexico, since I'll end up a queen, no matter what.
So you're just inventing all this crap.
No, I'm not, I swear. Well, some things, I embroider a little, so you can see them the way I did. But it was showing everywhere before the war was over.
Jesus, don't you know anything more recent?
Not with such exquisite romance and perfect beauty. They don't make them like that anymore.
Thank God.
You atheists never stop mentioning God.
And you fagots never face facts. Fantasies are no escape. Alienating yourself like that can drive you nuts, too, you know.
(points to door) If you've got the keys, I'll gladly follow. Otherwise I'll escape in my own way, thank you.
(disgusted) Then your life is as trivial as your movies. And so are you. I'm going to sleep).
Valentin blows out his candle. Molina, thinking deeply, blows out the other candle. The two candlewicks fade in the darkness.
Goodnight, Valentin. Dream about my French torch-singer.
Dream about your Gestapo goons.
INT. PRISON CORRIDOR - DAWN
FOUR GUARDS are conducting the morning bed check. As they open doors, we HEAR prisoners barking out their names. At the fourth door, we SEE the two men standing at attention.
Valentin Arregui!
Lewis Molina! The door is slammed shut.
INT. CELL - DAY
Molina mashes an avocado. Valentin reads in bed. Molina approaches with avocado crackers and two cups of tea.
Have some. Mother's cleaning out her cupboard. It's a long time till lunch.
No, I can't afford to get spoiled.
Mother of God, I offer you half of my precious avocado, and this is the thanks I get? You spit in my face!
Don't act like that. You get so damn sensitive.
That's how I am, very sentimental. (MORE)
You're so damn smart, tell me what to do about it.
I don't know, don't be so oversensitive. You sound just like a ...
Like a what? Say it, like a woman, that's what you meant, isn't it? (Valentin nods) What's wrong with being weak like a woman?
Women aren't weak, that's just your fantasy, and prejudice.
Why do only women get to be sensitive? Why not a man, a faggot, or any poor bastard? If more men acted like women, there wouldn't be so much violence.
Molina gestures at the welts on Valentin's arm.
You've got a point there. (looks up) But what would you do in a world with no men?
Touche. (sits down) You're right. Men are mostly brutes, but I love them.
Molina sips his tea and sees Valentin grimace.
Why do you look at me that way? You have no respect for me at all, do you? You think I'm just trash.
Look, Molina. I'm not feeling well. (indicates opposite cell) Who knows when it's my turn again.
So build your strength. avocado.
No thanks.
Do you really think eating this avocado will make you spoiled and weak? That much self-discipline is ridiculous. Relax. Enjoy what life offers you.
When you join the revolution, there's no time for pleasure. Gratifying the senses becomes secondary. The only thing I enjoy is serving a cause that is noble.
What kind of a cause is that? One that doesn't let you eat an avocado?
(smiles) Molina, you would never understand.
Try me.

Look, in order to live with myself, things must change in this country. Working for that is my only pleasure. And I can get that pleasure anywhere, right here in this cell, even during torture. If you want me to spell it out in one word, I'm a Marxist.
Marxist, Fascist, Capitalist. Who cares? They're all just fancy ways to hide your fear of pleasure. The only important thing is what you can see and touch. A hot cup
of tea, a soft bed, a warm lover.
I knew you wouldn't understand. Life is more important that a cup of tea.
Well, maybe you're right, but I only have only one life. How many do you have?
(bitter smile) Touche.
Valentin turns away, deep in thought.
INT. PRISON CORRIDOR - DAY
Two guards walk down the corridor and open a cell door. GUARD Let's go. The Warden wants to see
The man who steps out is Molina. He is led away.
EXT. PRISON COURTYARD - DAY

A massive wall with small barred windows faces the courtyard where prisoners are playing soccer. At one window, two hands reach up and grab the bars. Between the hands, Valentin's face slowly raises into view. Converted to PDF by www.screentalk.org
(stage whisper) Rafael. Can you hear me? Are you okay?
Valentin.
Are you okay?
The CAMERA PANS along the wall to the window of the next cell. Rafael, 38, has the withdrawn face of a political prisoner.
They won't let me sleep.
Who's the new guy across the hall?
I don't know. I can't take this much longer. I'm so afraid.
The CAMERA PANS back to Valentin's window.
We're all afraid.
No. I mean I'm afraid I'll talk.
Valentin's face fills with pain.
You'll make it, Rafael. stronger than them.
MONTAGE: PRISON ROUTINE

--- A new guard enters the watch tower. --- Prisoners sunbathe in the courtyard. --- Molina talks to the Warden. Converted to PDF by www.screentalk.org --- Prisoners mop the visiting-room floor. --- A guard shoves letters inside cell doors. --- Valentin picks up a letter and opens it. --- The sun sets behind the prison walls.
INT. CELL - NIGHT
Two bowls of black-bean stew are pushed through the food slot in their cell door. Molina gets up, but Valentin beats him to the door.
Great. I'm starving.
Valentin offers one bowl to Molina.
No, you take the big one. twice as much as yours.
It has
No, they want us to fight over it.
(insisting) But you need your strength.
Don't fight. That's what they want. Besides, I hate this crap.
Molina, extremely disturbed, accepts the larger portion. Valentin starts to eat and notices Molina toying with his food.
What's the matter? Not hungry?
No, no.
Molina swallows two spoonfuls of the stew. opens his letter while eating.
Converted to PDF by www.screentalk.org
Why won't you tell me who it's from? Your mother, your sweetheart? What's the big secret, anyway?
Valentin pockets the letter.
Why did the warden want to see you?
I told you. I may be paroled soon.
A lawyer tells you that. Warden. Not the

The Warden had just heard the rumor himself. It's far from definite. He called me in because my mother's doctor phoned from the hospital. She may be dying. I'd rather not talk about it. (sighs) Imagine the shame of having a son in prison. And the reason.
Finish your stew. It'll make you feel better. MOLINA No, only one thing can help, to tell my movie. Do you mind?
(grudgingly) Sure, man. Go ahead.
Man! Is there a man here? let him go. Where is he? Don't
(exasperated smile) Okay, cut the crap and tell your dumb movie.
Molina sets down his bowl and stands up.
Waiting in the moonlight behind
the cabaret is Werner's limousine. Long, sleek, shiny... with two crisp Nazi flags on the fenders. Werner's eyes are fixed on the stage door.
INT. LIMOUSINE - NIGHT (NAZI MOVIE)
Werner sees Leni and the Blonde step outside and wave goodbye. As Leni approaches the limousine, Werner opens the door.
Thank you for accepting my invitation.
(sitting down) I need a drink.
Werner motions the chauffeur to drive and opens the wine cabinet in his limo.
Nothing is better than Rhein wine. The finest in all Germany.
I prefer champagne.
It's French. Their eyes meet, testing each other's determination.
EXT. PARIS STREET - NIGHT (NAZI MOVIE)

The Blonde hurries along the wet sidewalk. her in the darkness. A car follows Converted to PDF by www.screentalk.org VOICE OF MOLINA Meanwhile, the Blonde hurries to meet a German Lieutenant, the father of her unborn child. But the Clubfoot has decided she is a traitor, maybe even a spy, because she cannot get a map to the secret German arsenal. The Blonde looks across the street and sees her lover in a second floor window. She steps off the curb. The car suddenly accelerates, and hurtles toward her. Turning in horror, she sees the Clubfoot at the wheel. The car races off into the darkness, leaving the Blonde's lifeless body sprawled on the wet pavement. We see the FACE of the horrified German Lieutenant in the window.
INT. CELL - NIGHT
Valentin's FACE: He is stunned but quickly hides it. Molina, caught up in his story, does not notice.
The smoke-filled restaurant is small, but extremely elegant...
INT. CHIC RESTAURANT - NIGHT (NAZI MOVIE)
Werner and Leni sip champagne, staring into each other's eyes.
(seductive smile) This French Resistance of yours can never do us real harm. But if it doesn't stop, it will destroy your country.
Perhaps. But nothing matters if France cannot be free. And it will be.
(laughs) I'm not offended. I like your arrogance. Everyone loves their country. If it were otherwise, I wouldn't trust you. (lifts his glass) Here's to a great artiste.
Leni lifts her glass.
Are you listening?
INT. CELL NIGHT
Valentin is lost in his own thoughts. Molina stands up.
You do this just to spit in my face, and you love it, don't you? Why do you humiliate me so? How can you leave me dangling like this, all alone, flapping my arms
like some empty scarecrow. Molina snaps his fingers near Valentin's face.
Strange. When the Blonde was killed, I -- . It was chilling.
Molina is elated to discover that Valentin's distraction is a tribute to his story-telling prowess.
It's only a movie, Valentin. Just one of Mother's many stories.
Converted to PDF by www.screentalk.org
Yeah. But I keep thinking about my friend. The same thing could happen to her.
Tell me about her. My lips are sealed.
I'm so helpless in here. way to protect her.
So you have a heart after all.
